schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch defend the fire from heaven as authentic history drawn from the Chronicler's source, not a later miracle-embellishment, and distinguish the glory that filled the house from the fire itself.

Henry reads the fire that fell on the prayer (not on the killing) as God's Amen — glorious in himself yet gracious to Israel, consuming the sacrifice offered in their stead, a type of Christ's death and the Spirit's descent.

Clarke: the cloud had come before, now the fire consumes the sacrifice, both house and offerings accepted; Wesley: the descent of holy fire is the surest evidence God accepts our prayers, the awed heart owned a living temple.
Gill fixes the moment: heaven answered the instant Solomon ended the prayer of the preceding chapter.

debates.question: Did the Chronicler invent the fire from heaven, or is the account authentic history?

sides.position: a later, unhistorical embellishment of the temple dedication
holders: Thenius and Bertheau (as reported by Keil and Delitzsch)

sides.position: authentic, drawn from one of the Chronicler's own sources

themes: type of Christ and the Spirit

schools.summary: Keil gives the plain cause — the priests could not enter because the glory filled the house — and Ellicott reads it as the second of two distinct theophanies (before and after the Prayer of Consecration), each overwhelming the priests as at 2 Chronicles 5:14 and 1 Kings 8:11.
Gill: the priests who had carried in the ark could not stand to minister, and came out unable to re-enter.

Ellicott: 'pavement' = ritspah, the LXX's lithostroton (cf. John 19:13); 'praised' = gave thanks; the refrain echoes the standing liturgy, almost the only phrase in the section characteristic of the Chronicler.
Wesley: the cloud came down upon the house then entered it, seen within by the priests and without by the people; the bowed faces express dread, submission, and unworthiness.

Henry: they did not flee the fire but kept their ground, adoring the glory and confessing 'He is good, for his mercy endureth for ever' — a song never out of season, since it is of the LORD's mercies they were not consumed.
Gill: the consuming fire was visible to the eye; the glory upon the house perhaps a distinct shining light.

themes: he is good, his mercy endureth
not consumed but the sacrifice in their stead

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'then' = and; 'offered sacrifices' is the durative 'were sacrificing a sacrifice' (LXX; Vulgate immolabant), picturing a sustained offering.
Clarke reconstructs the mechanics: the people presented (or slew) the victims, caught the blood and collected the fat, and gave them to the priests to offer as the law required.
Henry: they increased their sacrifices to feast the holy fire; God's favours should enlarge our service, and the king's example stirred the people, for good work goes on when leaders lead.
Gill joins the verse to the fuller account that follows.

Keil reads the great thank-offering as the dedication's close, tallying with 1 Kings 8:62-63; Ellicott secures the Hebrew and notes 'peace offerings' seems to have dropped from the text, the numbers identical in both accounts.
Clarke: the vast total is the sum of all victims over the fortnight — the seven days of tabernacles and seven of dedication — prodigious but not incredible.
Henry: the victims so outnumbered the altar's capacity that the overflow was burnt in the hallowed court, the pavement itself serving in necessity as an altar.

Ellicott: the whole verse is the Chronicler's own addition stressing the ritual and its music; 'waited on their offices' = stood at their posts; the Levites were David's ministers of praise, through whom his psalms still sounded.
Gill: the priests performed their offices, some sacrificing, others blowing the trumpets.
Wesley: David composed the psalms and appointed them to be sung by the Levites with instruments.
Henry: the singers used the instruments David made and the hymn he gave them; the work was accepted as his act, so the dead king still leads the living choir.
Keil reads v.7 as a pluperfect supplementary remark; Ellicott ties it to 1 Kings 8:64 and the altar of 2 Chronicles 4:1, noting burnt and meat offering stand in the collective singular.
Gill distinguishes the dedication of the altar from that of the house and adds a historical aside on heathen (and later Christian) dedication-feasts.
Henry: rather than turn back any sacrifice, the overflow was burnt in the hallowed court — in necessity the pavement itself may be an altar.

Keil notes the very great congregation from Hamath to the river of Egypt; Ellicott fixes 'the feast' as Tabernacles (Leviticus 23:34-36), its legal seven days counted from the fifteenth, and 'the river' as the wady of Egypt, with Kings adding 'before the LORD our God.'
Clarke hands on the Targum's 'from the entrance of Antioch to the Nile of Egypt.'

themes: from Hamath to the river of Egypt

schools.summary: Ellicott dates the eighth day to the twenty-second of Tisri, the solemn assembly of Leviticus 23:36, and disentangles the two seven-day festivals to explain Kings' 'fourteen days,' citing the Syriac; Keil reads the verse the same way.
Henry: we must never grudge the time we spend in the worship of God, nor think it long.
Clarke reconciles the twenty-third day (here) with the eighth (Kings) by Jarchi's staged dismissal, and unfolds the Targum's threefold thanksgiving — for David, for Solomon, for Israel.
Ellicott dates the day from the evening of the twenty-second and notes the Chronicler adds Solomon's name to Kings' 'David his servant.'
Henry: the people went home glad and merry, satisfied and not wearied by the many days of worship.

themes: goodness to David, Solomon, and Israel

schools.summary: Ellicott takes 'all that came into Solomon's heart' as the Chronicler's own addition ('all the desire,' cf. Isaiah 21:4; 2 Chronicles 8:6); Keil reads the verse as the seam closing the building-narrative.
Gill joins the notice to the opening of 1 Kings 9.
Henry: Solomon effected all he designed, the LORD's house named before his own — those who begin with God's service prosper in their own affairs, by grace.

themes: the LORD's house before the king's

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'by night' is implied in Kings; the Chronicler's addition to the prayer begins here, and 'house of sacrifice' occurs nowhere else in the Old Testament; Keil finds the two accounts agreeing in order, the Chronicler expounding more fully.
Clarke calls it a second manifestation (cf. 1 Kings 9:2-9) and gives the Targum's 'The Word of the LORD appeared to Solomon.'
Gill: the Chronicler adds a specific answer to the petitions on famine and pestilence, a specimen of God's readiness to hear and forgive.
Henry: a prayer may be accepted (by the fire) yet answered later in words; God owns the house as a house of sacrifice and of prayer for all people (Isaiah 56:7).

Ellicott roots each judgment in the covenant — drought (Deuteronomy 11:17; 2 Chronicles 6:26), the hagab locust (Leviticus 11:22), pestilence (2 Chronicles 6:28) — and reads the conditional 'if' as 'whether.'
Clarke gives the Targum's gloss on the pestilence: 'the angel of death.'
Henry: famine, pestilence, and (in the devouring locust) war are the deliberate instruments of a God who governs nations to bring a sinning people to their knees.

Ellicott grounds each clause in Scripture — 'called by my name' = dedicated to God (2 Chronicles 6:33; Jeremiah 14:9); 'humble,' 'seek my face,' 'turn' echo Leviticus 26:41, Psalms 27:8, Hosea 6:1 — the exact vocabulary of covenant return.

Henry unfolds the fourfold turning (humble, pray, seek, turn — reformation, not mere repentance) and the threefold mercy (hear, forgive, heal), noting pardoning mercy makes way for healing mercy (Psalms 103:3; Matthew 9:2).
Ellicott hears the promise echoing Solomon's own petition at 2 Chronicles 6:40 almost word for word — what the king asked, God now grants.
Clarke gives the Targum's reverent paraphrase, softening the divine eyes and ears into God's good pleasure and the mediating Word (Memra).
Henry: God engages to answer the prayers of his people made in this place, the open eyes and attentive ears a pledge that repentance will never meet silence.

themes: mine eyes open, mine ears attent

schools.summary: Ellicott sets 'chosen,' 'sanctified,' 'perpetually' beside their parallels (2 Chronicles 6:6; 1 Kings 9:3), 'perpetually' = 'all the days'; Keil reads it as part of the shared divine promise.
Henry: 'my name shall be there for ever' = there God makes himself known and there he is called upon — self-revelation and worship together.
Henry: the kingdom is perpetuated on condition of perseverance; to claim David's covenant Solomon must imitate David's walk — blessing and curse, life and death, set before him.
Ellicott notes Kings adds 'in integrity of heart' and the Chronicler's construction 'and to do' continuing a future; Keil reads the verse as the opening of the conditional promise.

themes: if thou wilt walk as David

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'as I have covenanted' is 'as I cut for' (the word covenant omitted, cf. 2 Chronicles 5:10), and 'ruler in Israel' substitutes a reminiscence of Micah 5:1 for Kings' 'throne of Israel'; Keil reads it as the renewal of the Davidic promise.
Clarke: 'there shall not fail thee a man' was not fulfilled because the condition was not — they forsook God and he cut off the throne.
Henry frames it as covenant-promise held out on condition of a David-like walk.
Ellicott: 'but' = and; the Hebrew implies 'ye and your children' has dropped (cf. 1 Kings 9:6), and the address shifts from the singular king (v.17) to the plural nation.
Henry: God supposed it possible that, though they had the temple, they might yet be drawn to other gods, knowing their proneness to backslide.
Ellicott: 'pluck up by the roots' refers to the children (v.19), the reverse of planting a nation (Jeremiah 24:6); the Chronicler softens Kings' 'cut off Israel.'
Clarke: how completely fulfilled — branches cut off, the very roots plucked up, neither root nor branch left.
Henry: though rooted deep and long in the good land, they would be extirpated like weeds thrown to the dunghill.

themes: the house cast from God's sight

schools.summary: Ellicott recovers the original reading 'ruinous heaps' (iyin, not elyon 'high'; cf. Micah 3:12), a corruption as old as the LXX; the passers-by shake the head and hiss.
Clarke: the ruin is astonishing — no nation so favoured, none so severely punished; the privilege measures the fall.

Henry: the sanctuary would be no sanctuary to shield them, against their vain boast 'the temple of the LORD are we' (Jeremiah 7:4); the house that was high would come down wonderfully (Lamentations 1:9).
Ellicott: 'it shall be answered' = men shall say (cf. Deuteronomy 29:24-25); the Chronicler writes 'he brought all this evil' where Kings names 'Jehovah' — the judgment self-interpreting.
Clarke: while they cleaved to God no enemy could touch them; forsaking him, the weakest foe oppressed them, for they conquered by divine power alone, and without it were worse than other men.

themes: grace and warning to the end
